narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which stands above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 45.2%—high enough to lead to the regrettable rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with the strongest projected odds of bar success. The most influential factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school raised concerns about sustaining high achievement through law school and the bar. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest favorable impact, actively pushing the prediction toward bar passage by demonstrating your college-level capability. Your LSAT score of 31, lower than the typical 36.7, also contributed somewhat toward the failure risk, though less forcefully than your Year 3 deciles. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 3 (below the average 5.7) and middle family income bracket each nudged the odds slightly higher for failure, while your full-time student status offered a small boost toward success, aligning with the norm for most applicants. Compared to typical students, your GPA shines brighter, but your school deciles and LSAT lag behind the pack, tipping the balance for this outcome. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework, and reapplying next cycle with updated achievements that highlight your resilience. You're capable of turning this around, and we're rooting for your success. Keep pushing forward.
